news_summary: Recent discoveries in space reveal potential new cosmic phenomena, including an unusual type of explosion and evidence suggesting a possible fifth force within our solar system.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes dans l'espace mettent en lumière de nouveaux phénomènes cosmiques potentiels, notamment un type inhabituel d'explosion et des indices suggérant l'existence d'une éventuelle cinquième force au sein de notre système solaire.
news_summary_es: Los recientes descubrimientos espaciales revelan posibles fenómenos cósmicos nuevos, entre ellos un tipo inusual de explosión y indicios que apuntan a la existencia de una posible quinta fuerza en nuestro sistema solar.

articles.title: Astronomers may have found a strange new kind of cosmic explosion
articles.summary: A mysterious cosmic explosion has astronomers buzzing, as a strange event may hint at an entirely new kind of stellar cataclysm. After detecting ripples in space-time, scientists spotted a fast-fading red glow that initially looked like a rare kilonova—the kind of collision that forges gold and uranium. But just days later, the signal shifted, behaving more like a supernova, leaving researchers puzzled. Now, some think they may have witnessed something never seen before: a “superkilonova.”
articles.content:
When very massive stars reach the ends of their lives, they explode as supernovae, scattering elements like carbon and iron across space. Another, rarer type of explosion happens when two neutron stars, the dense remnants of dead stars, collide. This event, known as a kilonova, produces even heavier elements such as gold and uranium. These materials are essential ingredients for forming stars, planets, and ultimately everything we see around us.

So far, scientists have confirmed only one clear example of a kilonova. That event, called GW170817, occurred in 2017 when two neutron stars merged. The collision sent out both gravitational waves and light, allowing researchers to observe it in multiple ways. The gravitational waves were detected by the National Science Foundation's Laser Interferometer Gravitational-wave Observatory (LIGO) and its European partner Virgo, while telescopes around the world captured the light from the explosion.

A New and Puzzling Cosmic Event

Astronomers now believe they may have found evidence of a second kilonova, although the situation is far from straightforward. The candidate event, named AT2025ulz, appears to be tied to a supernova that happened just hours earlier. That earlier explosion may have hidden key details, making the event much harder to interpret.

"At first, for about three days, the eruption looked just like the first kilonova in 2017," says Caltech's Mansi Kasliwal (PhD '11), professor of astronomy and director of Caltech's Palomar Observatory near San Diego. "Everybody was intensely trying to observe and analyze it, but then it started to look more like a supernova, and some astronomers lost interest. Not us."

Kasliwal led a study describing the findings in The Astrophysical Journal Letters. Her team suggests this unusual event could represent something entirely new, a superkilonova, meaning a kilonova triggered by a supernova. While scientists have proposed this idea before, it has never been observed.

Gravitational Waves Point to Something Unusual

The first sign of this rare event appeared on August 18, 2025. LIGO detectors in Louisiana and Washington, along with Virgo in Italy, recorded a new gravitational-wave signal. Within minutes, an alert went out to astronomers worldwide, noting that the signal likely came from two merging objects. At least one of those objects seemed unusually small. The alert also included a rough location in the sky.

"While not as highly confident as some of our alerts, this quickly got our attention as a potentially very intriguing event candidate," says David Reitze, the executive director of LIGO and a research professor at Caltech. "We are continuing to analyze the data, and it's clear that at least one of the colliding objects is less massive than a typical neutron star."

A few hours later, the Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) at Palomar Observatory identified a fading red source about 1.3 billion light years away, located in the same region as the gravitational-wave signal. Initially named ZTF 25abjmnps, the object was later given the official designation AT2025ulz.

A Signal That Changed Over Time

Roughly a dozen telescopes around the world quickly began observing the event, including the W. M. Keck Observatory in Hawaiʻi, the Fraunhofer telescope in Germany, and facilities connected to the GROWTH (Global Relay of Observatories Watching Transients Happen) program led by Kasliwal.

Early observations showed the object fading rapidly and glowing red, similar to what was seen in the 2017 kilonova. In that earlier event, the red color came from heavy elements like gold, which absorb blue light and allow red wavelengths to pass through.

However, the behavior of AT2025ulz soon changed. A few days after the initial flash, it brightened again, shifted to bluer light, and showed hydrogen in its spectra. These features are typical of a supernova, specifically a "stripped-envelope core-collapse" supernova, not a kilonova. Because supernovae in distant galaxies usually do not produce detectable gravitational waves, some astronomers concluded that the event was likely an ordinary supernova unrelated to the earlier signal.

Clues Point to a Possible Superkilonova

Kasliwal and her team noticed several signs that the event did not fit neatly into either category. AT2025ulz did not fully match the characteristics of a classic kilonova or a typical supernova. At the same time, gravitational-wave data suggested that at least one of the merging objects had a mass smaller than the Sun, raising the possibility that two unusually small neutron stars were involved.

Neutron stars are the dense remnants left behind after massive stars explode. They are roughly the size of San Francisco (about 25 kilometers across) and typically have masses between 1.2 and three times that of our Sun. Some theories suggest that even smaller neutron stars could exist, but none have been directly observed.

Scientists have proposed two ways such tiny neutron stars might form. In one scenario, a rapidly spinning massive star explodes and splits into two smaller neutron stars through a process called fission. In another, known as fragmentation, the explosion creates a disk of material around the collapsing core, and clumps in that disk eventually form a small neutron star, similar to how planets form.

A Hidden Collision Inside a Supernova

According to co author Brian Metzger of Columbia University, it is possible that two newly formed neutron stars could spiral inward and collide, producing a kilonova that emits gravitational waves. As this happens, the explosion would initially appear red due to the formation of heavy elements, just as telescopes observed. Meanwhile, debris from the earlier supernova could obscure the view, hiding the kilonova within it.

In simple terms, a supernova may have created two newborn neutron stars that quickly merged, producing a second explosion.

"The only way theorists have come up with to birth sub-solar neutron stars is during the collapse of a very rapidly spinning star," Metzger says. "If these 'forbidden' stars pair up and merge by emitting gravitational waves, it is possible that such an event would be accompanied by a supernova rather than be seen as a bare kilonova."

More Evidence Needed

Although this explanation is compelling, the researchers emphasize that it is still uncertain. There is not yet enough evidence to confirm that AT2025ulz is truly a superkilonova.

To test this idea, astronomers will need to identify more events like it. "Future kilonovae events may not look like GW170817 and may be mistaken for supernovae," Kasliwal says. "We can look for new possibilities in data like this from ZTF as well as the Vera Rubin Observatory, and upcoming projects such as NASA's Nancy Roman Space Telescope, NASA's UVEX [led by Caltech's Fiona Harrison], Caltech's Deep Synoptic Array-2000, and Caltech's Cryoscope in the Antarctic. We do not know with certainty that we found a superkilonova, but the event nevertheless is eye opening."

Study Details and Funding

The study, titled "ZTF25abjmnps (AT2025ulz) and S250818k: A Candidate Superkilonova from a Sub-threshold Sub-Solar Gravitational Wave Trigger," received funding from the Gordon and Betty Moore Foundation, the Knut and Alice Wallenberg Foundation, the National Science Foundation (NSF), the Simons Foundation, the US Department of Energy, a McWilliams Postdoctoral Fellowship, and the University of Ferrara in Italy. Other Caltech authors include Tomás Ahumada (now at NOIRLab, Chile), Viraj Karambelkar (now at Columbia University), Christoffer Fremling, Sam Rose, Kaustav Das, Tracy Chen, Nicholas Earley, Matthew Graham, George Helou, and Ashish Mahabal.

Caltech's ZTF is supported by the NSF and an international group of partners, with additional funding from the Heising-Simons Foundation and Caltech. ZTF data are processed and archived by IPAC, an astronomy center at Caltech.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the potential discovery of a new kind of cosmic explosion, a 'superkilonova,' which could represent a novel astrophysical phenomenon involving neutron stars and supernovae. This discovery has broad significance for understanding stellar evolution, element formation, and gravitational wave astronomy. The story is focused, detailed, and describes a real scientific breakthrough with wide implications for the general public's knowledge of the universe.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers may have discovered a new type of cosmic explosion called a superkilonova
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – When massive stars reach the end of their lives, they explode as supernovae, spreading elements like carbon and iron through space. Sometimes, two neutron stars—tiny, dense remains of dead stars—collide, creating a rare explosion called a kilonova. Kilonovae produce very heavy elements such as gold and uranium. Until now, scientists have only confirmed one clear kilonova event in 2017.

**What happened** – In August 2025, detectors in the United States and Italy recorded unusual gravitational waves, signals caused by massive objects merging in space. Soon after, telescopes spotted a red glow named AT2025ulz about 1.3 billion light years away. At first, it looked like a kilonova, but days later it changed, showing features like a supernova. This strange combination puzzled astronomers. A team led by Caltech’s Mansi Kasliwal suggests this could be a new kind of explosion—a “superkilonova”—where a kilonova happens inside a supernova.

**Impact** – This possible discovery is important because it challenges what scientists know about how stars die and how heavy elements form. A superkilonova would mean that two tiny neutron stars formed and collided inside a larger exploding star. This idea has never been observed before. It also shows that gravitational waves can come from different, unexpected sources, helping scientists learn more about the universe’s most powerful events.

**What’s next step** – Researchers want to find more events like AT2025ulz to confirm the existence of superkilonovae. Upcoming telescopes and observatories, like the Vera Rubin Observatory and NASA’s future space telescopes, will help monitor the sky for similar signals. Studying these events in more detail will improve understanding of how neutron stars form and merge, and how elements are created in space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Astronomers may have found a new cosmic explosion, a superkilonova, where two neutron stars merge inside a supernova, revealing a fresh way stars can end and create heavy elements.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes pourraient avoir découvert un nouveau type d’explosion cosmique appelée superkilonova
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Lorsque des étoiles massives arrivent en fin de vie, elles explosent en supernova, dispersant des éléments comme le carbone et le fer dans l’espace. Parfois, deux étoiles à neutrons — des restes très denses d’étoiles mortes — entrent en collision, créant une explosion rare appelée kilonova. Les kilonovae produisent des éléments très lourds comme l’or et l’uranium. Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques n’avaient confirmé qu’un seul événement clair de kilonova en 2017.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En août 2025, des détecteurs aux États-Unis et en Italie ont enregistré des ondes gravitationnelles inhabituelles, des signaux causés par la fusion d’objets massifs dans l’espace. Peu après, des télescopes ont repéré une lueur rouge nommée AT2025ulz à environ 1,3 milliard d’années-lumière. Au début, elle ressemblait à une kilonova, mais quelques jours plus tard, elle a changé et a montré des caractéristiques d’une supernova. Cette combinaison étrange a intrigué les astronomes. Une équipe dirigée par Mansi Kasliwal du Caltech suggère qu’il pourrait s’agir d’un nouveau type d’explosion — une « superkilonova » — où une kilonova se produit à l’intérieur d’une supernova.

**Impact** – Cette découverte possible est importante car elle remet en question ce que les scientifiques savent sur la fin de vie des étoiles et la formation des éléments lourds. Une superkilonova signifierait que deux petites étoiles à neutrons se sont formées et sont entrées en collision à l’intérieur d’une étoile en explosion. Cette idée n’a jamais été observée auparavant. Elle montre aussi que les ondes gravitationnelles peuvent provenir de sources différentes et inattendues, aidant les scientifiques à mieux comprendre les événements les plus puissants de l’univers.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent trouver d’autres événements comme AT2025ulz pour confirmer l’existence des superkilonovae. Les futurs télescopes et observatoires, comme le Vera Rubin Observatory et les futurs télescopes spatiaux de la NASA, aideront à surveiller le ciel pour détecter des signaux similaires. Étudier ces événements en détail améliorera la compréhension de la formation et de la fusion des étoiles à neutrons, ainsi que de la création des éléments dans l’espace.

**En une phrase** – Les astronomes pourraient avoir découvert une nouvelle explosion cosmique, une superkilonova, où deux étoiles à neutrons fusionnent à l’intérieur d’une supernova, révélant une nouvelle façon dont les étoiles se terminent et créent des éléments lourds.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astrónomos podrían haber descubierto un nuevo tipo de explosión cósmica llamada superkilonova
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cuando las estrellas muy grandes llegan al final de su vida, explotan como supernovas, esparciendo elementos como carbono y hierro por el espacio. A veces, dos estrellas de neutrones — restos muy densos de estrellas muertas — chocan, creando una explosión rara llamada kilonova. Las kilonovas producen elementos muy pesados como el oro y el uranio. Hasta ahora, los científicos solo habían confirmado un evento claro de kilonova en 2017.

**Qué pasó** – En agosto de 2025, detectores en Estados Unidos e Italia registraron ondas gravitacionales inusuales, señales causadas por la fusión de objetos muy masivos en el espacio. Minutos después, telescopios detectaron un brillo rojo llamado AT2025ulz a unos 1.3 mil millones de años luz. Al principio parecía una kilonova, pero días después cambió y mostró características de una supernova. Esta combinación extraña desconcertó a los astrónomos. Un equipo dirigido por Mansi Kasliwal de Caltech sugiere que podría ser un nuevo tipo de explosión: una “superkilonova”, donde una kilonova ocurre dentro de una supernova.

**Impacto** – Este posible descubrimiento es importante porque desafía lo que los científicos saben sobre cómo mueren las estrellas y cómo se forman los elementos pesados. Una superkilonova significaría que dos estrellas de neutrones muy pequeñas se formaron y chocaron dentro de una estrella que explotaba. Esta idea nunca se había observado antes. También muestra que las ondas gravitacionales pueden venir de fuentes diferentes e inesperadas, ayudando a los científicos a aprender más sobre los eventos más poderosos del universo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren encontrar más eventos como AT2025ulz para confirmar la existencia de las superkilonovas. Próximos telescopios y observatorios, como el Observatorio Vera Rubin y futuros telescopios espaciales de la NASA, ayudarán a vigilar el cielo en busca de señales similares. Estudiar estos eventos con más detalle mejorará el entendimiento sobre cómo se forman y fusionan las estrellas de neutrones y cómo se crean los elementos en el espacio.

**Una frase para recordar** – Los astrónomos podrían haber encontrado una nueva explosión cósmica, una superkilonova, donde dos estrellas de neutrones se fusionan dentro de una supernova, mostrando una forma nueva en que las estrellas terminan y crean elementos pesados.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two glowing, dense star-like spheres spiraling close together inside a vibrant, colorful cosmic explosion blending red and blue hues, symbolizing a rare merger of newborn neutron stars within a supernova’s swirling debris cloud, with subtle hints of shimmering gold and uranium-like sparks radiating outward against a soft, dark space backdrop.

articles.title: NASA scientist says a "fifth force" may be hiding in our solar system
articles.summary: Scientists are grappling with a cosmic mystery: why does the Universe behave differently on massive scales compared to our own solar system? While distant galaxies reveal clear signs of something bending the rules of gravity—often attributed to dark energy or a hidden “fifth force”—everything nearby seems to follow Einstein’s playbook perfectly.
articles.content:
Science advances by proposing ideas and then trying to prove them wrong. This process becomes especially challenging when dealing with the Universe on its largest scales. Dark energy and dark matter are among the most difficult concepts to test. Observations across vast regions of space clearly suggest that something is influencing gravity in ways Einstein's theory does not fully explain. Yet within our own solar system, everything appears to behave exactly as expected.

A new study by Slava Turyshev, a physicist at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory, explores how researchers might address this mismatch. His work suggests that the key may lie in being extremely precise and selective in how experiments are designed to search for signs of dark energy and dark matter closer to home.

The "Great Disconnect" Between Cosmic and Local Physics

At the center of the problem is what scientists call the "Great Disconnect." The laws of physics seem to operate differently depending on the scale being observed. In regions with very little matter (i.e. no gravitational force), the effects linked to dark energy or modified gravity become much more noticeable. In contrast, in dense environments filled with matter and strong gravity, those same effects seem to vanish, at least based on current instruments.

Within the solar system, everything aligns with traditional physics. Planets follow their expected orbits. Measurements of spacetime around the Sun, including data from spacecraft signals, match predictions precisely. Every probe sent through the solar system behaves as if only standard gravity is at work. There are no clear signs of anything unusual.

Strong Evidence From the Distant Universe

The situation changes dramatically when looking far beyond our local neighborhood. On the scale of galaxies and beyond, the Universe appears to be expanding. While scientists continue to debate the exact rate of this expansion, there is strong evidence that something is influencing gravity or spacetime in ways not fully captured by current theories.

At present, dark energy is the best explanation for this behavior, even though its true nature remains unknown.

Screening Effects and the Hidden "Fifth Force"

One possible explanation involves a phenomenon known as "screening." In this idea, whatever is causing the discrepancy changes how it behaves depending on the surrounding environment. As density increases, its effects become weaker or harder to detect.

There are two main types of screening models. The first is called the "chameleon" model. In this scenario, a hypothetical fifth force of nature (other than gravity, electromagnetism, and the two nuclear forces) adjusts its strength based on the amount of nearby matter. In low density regions, it becomes strong and produces effects associated with dark energy. In dense areas, it weakens so much that current instruments cannot detect it, even though it still exists. Around objects like the Sun, it might only appear in a thin outer layer, but in principle it could still be measured there.

Vainshtein Screening and Suppressed Forces

Another explanation is the Vainshtein screening model. Here, the force itself does not change. Instead, the surrounding gravity effectively suppresses its influence, making it appear weak. The model introduces the concept of a Vainshtein Radius, which marks the distance where the force regains its normal strength.

For the Sun, this radius is estimated to extend about 400 light years. That region includes many stars, meaning the force would remain suppressed well beyond the solar system and even across large parts of the galaxy.

Why New Solar System Missions May Be Needed

Both screening models could leave subtle traces in large-scale observations collected by missions such as Euclid and The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI). However, these surveys focus on distant galaxies and cannot directly reveal how such forces behave within the solar system.

To test these ideas locally, scientists would need a dedicated mission designed specifically for that purpose. Even more important, researchers would need a falsifiable theory that predicts what such a mission should detect.

The Importance of Testable Predictions

Dr. Turyshev emphasizes that without a clear, testable prediction, additional experiments in the solar system are unlikely to yield new results. So far, observations have consistently confirmed general relativity. Continuing to run similar experiments without new theoretical guidance may not provide useful insights.

However, if scientists can use data from large cosmological surveys to develop precise hypotheses that apply to the solar system, then it becomes possible to design targeted experiments to test them.

Looking Ahead: Building Better Instruments

It may take time to develop instruments sensitive enough to detect these subtle effects. In the meantime, incremental progress will be important, with missions focused on improving measurement capabilities step by step.

If a well-defined and testable prediction emerges from current data, and if an experiment can realistically be built to test it, pursuing that opportunity could lead to a major breakthrough. Such a discovery has the potential to reshape our understanding of gravity, dark energy, and the fundamental workings of the Universe.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses a significant scientific inquiry into a potential 'fifth force' in physics that could fundamentally reshape our understanding of gravity and the Universe. This research has broad implications for cosmology and physics, addressing a major cosmic mystery with potential large-scale benefits for humanity's knowledge of the universe. It focuses on a single topic with detailed explanations of the scientific models and the need for future missions, showing substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: NASA scientist explores possibility of a hidden 'fifth force' in our solar system
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have noticed that the Universe behaves differently on very large scales compared to what we see in our own solar system. While galaxies far away show unusual effects that suggest there might be forces beyond what Einstein's gravity explains, everything close to us seems to follow the known rules perfectly.

**What happened** – A NASA physicist named Slava Turyshev studied this difference, called the "Great Disconnect." He looked into how a possible "fifth force" might exist but hide near us because of the way it changes strength depending on the environment. Two main ideas, called "chameleon" and "Vainshtein" screening, explain how this force could be strong in empty space but weak or hidden near dense objects like the Sun. Turyshev suggests that new, very precise space missions might be needed to find signs of this force inside our solar system.

**Impact** – This research is important because it tries to explain a big mystery: why gravity seems different across the Universe. If a fifth force exists, it could change how we understand everything from the movement of galaxies to the expansion of the Universe. Finding this force near us would help connect what we see in space with the physics we test on Earth and nearby planets.

**What's next step** – Scientists need to develop clear predictions about what to look for in the solar system and design special missions with instruments sensitive enough to detect tiny effects of this hidden force. Progress will be slow but steady, focusing on improving tools and experiments to test these ideas.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA research suggests a hidden fifth force may exist but is difficult to detect near Earth, requiring new experiments to better understand gravity across the Universe.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un scientifique de la NASA explore la possibilité d'une « cinquième force » cachée dans notre système solaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques ont remarqué que l’Univers se comporte différemment à très grande échelle comparé à ce que nous observons dans notre propre système solaire. Alors que les galaxies lointaines montrent des effets inhabituels qui suggèrent l’existence de forces au-delà de la gravité d’Einstein, tout près de nous semble suivre parfaitement les règles connues.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un physicien de la NASA, Slava Turyshev, a étudié cette différence appelée « Grande déconnexion ». Il a examiné comment une possible « cinquième force » pourrait exister mais se cacher près de nous à cause de la façon dont elle change de force selon l’environnement. Deux idées principales, appelées « effet caméléon » et « effet Vainshtein », expliquent comment cette force pourrait être forte dans l’espace vide mais faible ou cachée près d’objets denses comme le Soleil. Turyshev suggère que de nouvelles missions spatiales très précises pourraient être nécessaires pour détecter cette force dans notre système solaire.

**Impact** – Cette recherche est importante car elle tente d’expliquer un grand mystère : pourquoi la gravité semble différente à travers l’Univers. Si une cinquième force existe, cela pourrait changer notre compréhension des mouvements des galaxies et de l’expansion de l’Univers. Trouver cette force près de nous aiderait à relier ce que nous voyons dans l’espace avec la physique testée sur Terre et près des planètes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques doivent élaborer des prédictions claires sur ce qu’il faut chercher dans le système solaire et concevoir des missions spéciales avec des instruments assez sensibles pour détecter de petits effets de cette force cachée. Les progrès seront lents mais réguliers, en se concentrant sur l’amélioration des outils et des expériences pour tester ces idées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une recherche de la NASA suggère qu’une cinquième force cachée pourrait exister mais est difficile à détecter près de la Terre, nécessitant de nouvelles expériences pour mieux comprendre la gravité dans l’Univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científico de la NASA explora la posibilidad de una 'quinta fuerza' oculta en nuestro sistema solar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han notado que el Universo se comporta de manera diferente en escalas muy grandes comparado con lo que vemos en nuestro propio sistema solar. Mientras que galaxias lejanas muestran efectos inusuales que sugieren fuerzas más allá de la gravedad de Einstein, todo cerca de nosotros parece seguir las reglas conocidas perfectamente.

**Qué pasó** – Un físico de la NASA llamado Slava Turyshev estudió esta diferencia llamada la "Gran Desconexión." Investigó cómo una posible "quinta fuerza" podría existir pero estar oculta cerca de nosotros porque cambia su fuerza según el entorno. Dos ideas principales, llamadas "modelo camaleón" y "modelo Vainshtein," explican cómo esta fuerza podría ser fuerte en el espacio vacío pero débil o escondida cerca de objetos densos como el Sol. Turyshev sugiere que se necesitan nuevas misiones espaciales muy precisas para detectar señales de esta fuerza dentro de nuestro sistema solar.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación es importante porque intenta explicar un gran misterio: por qué la gravedad parece diferente en todo el Universo. Si existe una quinta fuerza, podría cambiar cómo entendemos desde el movimiento de las galaxias hasta la expansión del Universo. Encontrar esta fuerza cerca de nosotros ayudaría a conectar lo que vemos en el espacio con la física que probamos en la Tierra y planetas cercanos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos deben crear predicciones claras sobre qué buscar en el sistema solar y diseñar misiones especiales con instrumentos sensibles para detectar efectos pequeños de esta fuerza oculta. El progreso será lento pero constante, enfocándose en mejorar herramientas y experimentos para probar estas ideas.

**Resumen en una frase** – La investigación de la NASA sugiere que podría existir una quinta fuerza oculta difícil de detectar cerca de la Tierra, por lo que se necesitan nuevos experimentos para entender mejor la gravedad en el Universo.
articles.image_prompt: A serene cosmic landscape showing the Sun at the center surrounded by precise orbits of planets, with a subtle, translucent ripple or wave pattern emanating outward symbolizing a hidden "fifth force" gently interacting with the solar system, blending softly into a distant starry background that hints at vast galaxies beyond, all rendered in warm, natural tones with delicate, detailed brushstrokes.
